questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When is a body said to be positively charged?

options.A: When it has more electrons than protons
options.B: When it has fewer electrons than protons
options.C: When it has equal numbers of protons and electrons

questions.hint: Positive charge means deficiency of electrons.
questions.explanation: A body becomes positively charged when it loses electrons, resulting in fewer electrons than protons. Option B correctly states this; A describes negative charge; C describes neutral.

source_trace.content_anchor: When do you say that a body is charged positively?

explanations_tiered.definition: A positively charged body has a deficit of electrons (more protons than electrons).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Charge is determined by the imbalance between protons and electrons. Losing electrons creates positive charge.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Positive charge means the object has lost some negative electrons, so it has extra positive protons.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: If an object has fewer electrons than protons, the net charge is positive. This occurs through electron loss via rubbing or conduction.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The elementary charge e = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C. Positive charge Q = +ne where n is the number of electrons lost.
reasoning_steps: Recall that protons are positive, electrons negative.
Positive means more protons than electrons (fewer electrons).
misconceptions: Some think positive charge means extra protons are added; usually it means electrons are removed.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Positive charge = fewer electrons than protons.

glossary_entry.definition: Condition of having fewer electrons than protons.

flashcard.front: How can you tell a body is positively charged?
flashcard.back: It has fewer electrons than protons.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: When is a body positively charged?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: When it has fewer electrons than protons.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize electron transfer, not proton movement.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Positive charge means fewer electrons than protons.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When a charged object is brought near a stream of smoke rising from a wooden splint, the smoke stream is attracted to the object. Why does this happen?

options.A: Smoke particles are initially charged and repel the object
options.B: The charged object induces opposite charges on smoke particles, causing attraction
options.D: The object heats the smoke, causing it to rise

questions.explanation: Smoke particles are neutral but contain polar molecules or can have charges induced on them. The charged object induces an opposite charge on the near side of smoke particles, causing electrostatic attraction. Option B is correct.

source_trace.content_anchor: Charge an object by friction and bring it near a stream of smoke rising from a wooden splint. What do you see? Explain why it happens.

explanations_tiered.definition: Induction causes neutral particles to polarize and be attracted to a charged object.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The charged object repels electrons (or attracts them) in the neutral smoke particles, creating an induced dipole. The near side has opposite charge, causing attraction.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The charged object pulls opposite charges to the near side of smoke particles, like a magnet attracting paper clips that aren't magnetic.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Smoke particles contain polar molecules and mobile charges. The external field from the charged object induces charge separation, leading to an attractive force (polarization force).
explanations_tiered.advanced: This is electrostatic induction in dielectric particles. The force is always attractive regardless of the sign of the charging object because induced dipoles align with the field.
reasoning_steps: Field induces charge separation in neutral smoke particles.
Opposite charges attract, causing smoke to bend toward object.
misconceptions: Smoke is not initially charged; induction causes temporary polarization.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Charged object attracts neutral smoke by induction.

glossary_entry.definition: Attraction of neutral particles to a charged object via polarization.

flashcard.front: Why does a charged object attract a stream of smoke?
flashcard.back: Induction polarizes smoke particles, causing attraction.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why does charged object attract smoke stream?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Induction polarizes smoke particles, causing attraction.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Charged object attracts neutral smoke by induction.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate with a charged rod and incense smoke.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Induction causes opposite charges on smoke particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: How can you determine whether an electroscope is charged?

options.B: The gold leaves will diverge (spread apart)
options.D: The glass container will change color

questions.explanation: When an electroscope has a net charge, the gold leaves acquire the same sign of charge and repel each other, causing them to diverge. Option B is the correct indicator; parallel leaves suggest neutrality.

source_trace.content_anchor: How do you know that whether or not an electroscope is charged?

explanations_tiered.definition: A charged electroscope shows leaf divergence due to like-charge repulsion.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When the electroscope has a net charge, both leaves acquire the same sign, repel, and spread apart. The angle indicates relative charge magnitude.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If the gold leaves are spread apart like an open book, the electroscope has charge. If they hang straight down, it's neutral.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Leaf divergence occurs because like charges repel. The leaves are both positive or both negative, so they push apart.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The electroscope can be charged by conduction or induction. Leaf divergence angle θ is proportional to charge for small angles (within limits).
misconceptions: Sometimes leaves diverge due to induction without net charge; but permanent divergence requires net charge.

glossary_entry.definition: Spreading of electroscope leaves due to like-charge repulsion.

flashcard.front: How can you tell an electroscope is charged?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How to know if electroscope is charged?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Gold leaves diverge due to repulsion.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show with real or simulated electroscope.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Diverging leaves indicate charge.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Why is it rare for a person inside a car to be struck by lightning?

options.A: Cars are made of insulating rubber
options.B: The metal body diverts current around the outside (Faraday cage effect)
options.C: Cars have lightning rods built into the roof
options.D: Cars are too small to attract lightning

questions.hint: The metal shell acts as a protective shield.
questions.explanation: A car's metal body acts as a Faraday cage: lightning current flows around the outside of the metal shell, not through the interior. Option B correctly explains this protection; tires are not the primary factor.

source_trace.content_anchor: Have you heard about an individual's death through lightning? It is very rare that people are struck by lightning and certainly you will not be struck while you are inside a car... The metal shell around you would divert charge away.

explanations_tiered.definition: A car acts as a Faraday cage, conducting lightning current around its exterior.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The metal body provides a low-resistance path for lightning current, directing it to the ground without passing through the interior.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The metal car body acts like a shield: lightning flows around the outside, so people inside are safe.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: A Faraday cage distributes charge on its outer surface. The electric field inside the conducting shell is zero, protecting occupants.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The principle: conductors in electrostatic equilibrium have no internal field. The car's metal body, even if not perfectly closed, diverts the majority of the current.
reasoning_steps: Current flows through metal body (low resistance).
Current does not enter interior due to Faraday cage effect.
misconceptions: Rubber tires do not protect from lightning; the metal body does.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Car = Faraday cage: lightning flows around outside.

glossary_entry.definition: Enclosure that blocks external electric fields and diverts current around the outside.

flashcard.front: Why is a car a safe place during lightning?
flashcard.back: Metal body diverts current around outside (Faraday cage effect).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is a car safe during lightning?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Metal body acts as Faraday cage, diverting current around outside.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Metal enclosure that shields interior from electric fields.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Metal body diverts current (Faraday cage effect).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two charges +1 C and -4 C exist in air. What is the direction of the force between them?

options.D: Depends on which charge is larger

questions.explanation: Opposite charges (positive and negative) attract each other. The force is directed toward each other regardless of magnitude. Option B is correct.

source_trace.content_anchor: Two charges 1C and -4C exists in air. What is the direction of force between them?

explanations_tiered.definition: Opposite charges attract; like charges repel.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The force direction is along the line joining the charges. Positive and negative charges always attract each other.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Positive and negative are opposites, so they pull toward each other like magnets.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Coulomb's law gives magnitude; direction is attractive for opposite signs. Force on +1 C is toward the -4 C, and vice versa.
explanations_tiered.advanced: By Newton's third law, forces are equal and opposite. The direction is determined by \(\mathbf{F}_{12} = k q_1 q_2 \hat{\mathbf{r}}_{12}/r^2\); with opposite signs, \(\hat{\mathbf{r}}_{12}\) direction gives attraction.
reasoning_steps: Thus force is attractive (toward each other).
misconceptions: Some think larger charge determines direction; both experience force toward each other.

flashcard.front: Direction of force between +1 C and -4 C?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Force direction between +1 C and -4 C?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What is the direction of the electric field due to a positive point charge?

options.D: No direction; it is a scalar

questions.hint: Electric field direction is the direction of force on a positive test charge.
questions.explanation: A positive test charge would be repelled by a positive point charge, so the field points radially outward. Option B is correct; inward is for negative charges.

source_trace.content_anchor: What is the direction of the electric field due to a positive point charge?

explanations_tiered.definition: Electric field direction is defined as the direction of force on a positive test charge.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For a positive source charge, a positive test charge experiences repulsion, so the field points away from the source.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a positive charge pushing away a tiny positive tester. The push direction is outward, away from the charge.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(\mathbf{E} = (kq/r^2) \hat{\mathbf{r}}\). For q > 0, \(\hat{\mathbf{r}}\) is radial outward. For q < 0, inward.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The field vector is radial due to spherical symmetry. The sign of q determines outward (positive) or inward (negative).
reasoning_steps: Recall definition: E = F/q₀ with q₀ positive.
Positive source charge repels positive test charge.
Force on test charge is away from source.
misconceptions: Confusing with negative charge field direction.

glossary_entry.definition: Direction of force on a positive test charge.

flashcard.front: Direction of E-field from positive point charge?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: E-field points away from positive charge.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Field points radially outward from positive charge.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which circuit symbol represents a switch in the closed (ON) position?

options.B: A circle with a cross inside
options.C: Two circles with a line connecting them
options.D: A gap in a line with a filled circle at one end

questions.hint: A closed switch symbol connects two points.
questions.explanation: In circuit diagrams, a closed switch is typically drawn as two small circles connected by a line (or as a simple line connection). Option C matches the conventional closed switch symbol from Table 4.1; an open switch has a gap.

source_trace.content_anchor: Switch symbol: two circles with connecting line (closed) or gap (open)

explanations_tiered.definition: A closed switch symbol indicates a complete conducting path.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Table 4.1 shows the switch symbol as two small circles with a line between them when closed (ON).
explanations_tiered.beginner: The closed switch symbol looks like two dots connected by a line — like a bridge that lets electricity cross.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In schematic diagrams, a closed switch is drawn as a line connecting two points (or with circles at ends). An open switch has a gap.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Switch symbols vary by standard (ANSI vs IEC), but the closed state always provides continuity between terminals.
reasoning_steps: Symbol: two circles with connecting line.
misconceptions: Confusing switch symbol with resistor or bulb.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Closed switch symbol: two circles with line.

glossary_entry.definition: Circuit symbol for a switch; closed = connected line.

flashcard.front: What is the symbol for a closed switch?
flashcard.back: Two small circles with a line connecting them.

visual_system.visual_description: Table showing switch symbol, battery symbol, resistor symbol, bulb symbol, ammeter, voltmeter.
visual_system.caption: Table 4.1: Electrical component symbols.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Table of circuit symbols: switch, battery, resistor, bulb, ammeter, voltmeter.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Two circles with a line connecting them.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Two circles connected by a line.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Two circles with line connecting them represents closed switch.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: How much work is done to move a charge of 2.0 C across a potential difference of 12 V?
questions.explanation: Work done \(W = Q \Delta V = 2.0 \times 12 = 24\) J. Option C is correct; A uses division, B uses one factor only, D uses 3×12.

source_trace.content_anchor: W = ΔV × Q = 12 V × 2 C = 24 J

explanations_tiered.definition: Work done to move charge through a potential difference is \(W = Q\Delta V\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: One volt is one joule per coulomb. Multiplying charge (C) by voltage (V) gives work in joules.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If 1 volt means 1 joule per coulomb, then 2 coulombs at 12 volts needs 2 × 12 = 24 joules.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From definition \(\Delta V = W/Q\), rearranged \(W = Q\Delta V = 2.0 \times 12 = 24\) J.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This is electrical potential energy change. Work is independent of path, depends only on endpoints and charge.
misconceptions: Using W = ΔV/Q instead of W = QΔV.

flashcard.front: Work to move 2 C across 12 V?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: 4.9 Combination of resistors in a circuit
questions.question: How many different paths can electric current follow in a series circuit?
questions.hint: Series circuits have a single loop.
questions.explanation: A series circuit has exactly one path for current to flow. If that path is broken, current stops entirely. Option B is correct.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.9 Combination of resistors in a circuit
source_trace.content_anchor: How many different paths can electric current follow in a series circuit?

explanations_tiered.definition: A series circuit has only one conducting path for current.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: All components are connected end-to-end, forming a single loop. Current is the same everywhere.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Series circuit is like a single-lane road: only one path from start to finish.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: There is exactly one route for electrons to travel from the negative terminal to the positive terminal through all components.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Kirchhoff's current law at any node in a series circuit: I_in = I_out, with no branching.
reasoning_steps: All components in a single loop.
misconceptions: Confusing series with parallel (which has multiple paths).

glossary_entry.definition: Single current path in a series circuit.

flashcard.front: Number of current paths in a series circuit?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Number of current paths in series?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Series circuits have exactly one path.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Series circuits have one current path.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: 4.11 Electrical safety in general and local context
questions.question: What three factors determine the severity of damage caused by an electric shock to the human body?

options.B: Current magnitude, duration of contact, and path through the body
options.C: Resistance of skin only, age, and gender

questions.hint: The severity depends on how much current, how long, and where it goes.
questions.explanation: The degree of damage depends on the magnitude of the current, the length of time it acts, and the part of the body through which it passes. Option B correctly lists these three factors.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.11 Electrical safety in general and local context
source_trace.content_anchor: Students, try to identify what determines the damage caused to the human body by an electric shock. The degree of damage to the body depends on the magnitude of the current, the length of time it acts, and the part of the body through which it passes.

explanations_tiered.definition: Shock severity depends on current magnitude, duration, and current path through the body.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Higher current causes more damage; longer exposure increases risk; path through vital organs (heart) is most dangerous.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A big current, a long time, or a path through your heart makes an electric shock very dangerous.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Even small currents (≈100 mA) can be fatal if they pass through the heart for more than a second. Hand-to-hand path is especially dangerous.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Cardiac vulnerability: currents as low as 50–100 mA through the thorax can cause ventricular fibrillation. The let-go threshold is ~10 mA.
reasoning_steps: Current magnitude (I) determines heating and muscle effects.
Path determines which organs are affected.
misconceptions: Voltage alone does not determine severity; body resistance varies.

glossary_entry.definition: Current magnitude, duration, and path through body determine shock danger.

flashcard.front: What three factors affect electric shock severity?
flashcard.back: Current magnitude, duration, and path through body.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: List three factors determining shock severity.
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Current magnitude, duration, path through body.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Determined by I, t, and path.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize why even low voltage can be dangerous if skin is wet.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Current magnitude, duration, and path through body.
